With two things.

I don't think we would describe ourselves as a BI tool in and of itself.
It feels like we're on a collision course there and, you know, but we haven't like fully aggressively stepped into that over that chasm, I guess.
So this was like a real confluence of, and I guess probably a third thing is that the enterprise segment is a segment where we have enterprise customers.

On the product marketing side, from two different people, they said I was getting feedback along the lines of, like, I don't know if we're saying anything here or, like, what we have to say in here, which I'm working with them to flesh that feedback out a little more so we can actually operationalize that.
But one thing I'm hoping you can help with is, like, what is, what would you tell them for, like, the value of, you know, comparing these two tools, like, Hex is not, like, the center of this story.

Like, how does that come back to us, whether that's citations or traffic, and then people realize, actually, Hex is the right tool, or this is supporting our authority in the BI space, and there's other BI surfaces on the site that will benefit from this.
Like, I'm not in a position where I feel like we need to sell this, but, like, if I were to need to sell this or defend this, I found myself having, like, loose ideas of why.
But maybe wanting a more concrete answer for like, is, you know, how are going to tell if this is worth it, if this matters, if this is a good piece of content for us to be focusing on?

Yeah, yeah, this is, we actually had a very similar question come up from a new customer we started working with yesterday, they were like, why do we want to, why would we publish content about competitors that and we're not in the title or like, we're just not the center of the story.
And it really comes down to being part of the conversation and just capturing traffic from a qualified audience.
So it's, you know, reasonable to assume that a lot of the people who would be comparing Tableau and Power BI, a considerable number would also be interested in Hex.
And this way, you're kind of like, capturing people that might not know about Hex, but they know about these tools.
And it's kind of like,
Trying to siphon that attention that would otherwise go to your competitors, that would go to Hex.
So, and it's not, this is not a like unique approach.
You have like hundreds of like businesses do this, like SEO play where you're essentially talking about your like competitors.
You're not like positioning yourself overtly in the title, but you are able to capture that search traffic.
And like more importantly, like get in front of people who don't know about you, but they're still like a qualified audience.
So that would be, and it's also like making you, it's also like teaching from like an AI search perspective.
It's also kind of instructing LLMs how to think about Hex versus these other tools.
I think one of the risks that comes with not having a lot of like comparison content on your site is that you are essentially...
Allowing competitors and like third party websites to define Hex in relation to your top competitors.
And in that scenario, the best case scenario is that it's factually accurate, but it's not like truly playing up your strengths.
It's not showing your benefits.
They might use like outdated positioning or, you know, like highlight maybe benefits that are no longer like a key part.
You're offering the worst case scenario is that they could be like straight up based on lies or just like misconceptions or like just like criticism that kind of builds over time.
And with if you're not part of that story, it's kind of hard to steer the conversation.
So if you ask me, this is always this type of content is always a good idea.
It's helpful to your audience.
Like you might not be at the center of the conversation, but it's still helpful for your ICP.
And I think it's you should have it on your site.
